senses.definition: to argue and express one's disagreement or objection to something

examples.x: The employees gathered to remonstrate against the sudden changes in their work hours.
The citizens remonstrated against the construction of the new highway.
The students remonstrated with the administration over the strict dress code policy.
The community members remonstrated against the construction of a new landfill in their residential area.
When the employees learned about the proposed pay cuts, they remonstrated with the management.

senses.definition: to express unhappiness in an irritable manner

examples.x: Despite the beautiful weather, John continued to grouch about having to work on a Saturday.
The old man would grouch about the noise from the construction site next door every morning.
Instead of enjoying the family gathering, Uncle Bob spent the entire time grouching about various issues.
Whenever there's a delay in public transportation, passengers tend to grouch about the inconvenience.

senses.definition: to complain or whine persistently and often about trivial matters

examples.x: She tends to kvetch about the weather, no matter what it's like.
The employee would kvetch regularly about office policies.
The customer continued to kvetch about the service, despite the efforts to resolve issues.
Don't kvetch about the traffic; we'll get there eventually.

senses.definition: to complain or criticize persistently, often about trivial issues

examples.x: Jane was always carping about her coworkers' minor mistakes in the past.
Critics seem to carp endlessly about plot holes no one else even notices.
At the meeting tomorrow, I hope no one will carp about typos in the report again.

senses.definition: to argue over unimportant things or to complain about them

examples.x: Instead of focusing on the main issue, he chose to quibble over minor details.
They spent hours quibbling over the wording of the contract, delaying its completion.
She didn't want to quibble about the cost; she just wanted the project to be completed on time.
The children often quibbled over trivial things like who got to sit in the front seat of the car.
Instead of offering constructive feedback, he just quibbled about every aspect of the presentation.

senses.definition: to strongly and harshly criticize someone or something

examples.x: The manager castigated the employee for consistently failing to meet deadlines.
The journalist castigated the government in the editorial for its handling of the crisis.
Unhappy with the product quality, the customer castigated the company in the online review.
The professor castigated the students for their lack of preparation in the final exam.
He was castigating his employees for not meeting the company's standards.

senses.definition: to find fault or criticize small, insignificant details

examples.x: She tends to nitpick about grammar errors in written documents.
Instead of enjoying the movie, he chose to nitpick about unrealistic plot points.
Don't nitpick every decision; sometimes, it's okay to go with the flow.
He continued to nitpick the painting, overlooking its overall beauty.
Despite their success, critics were quick to nitpick the flaws in the new technology.

senses.definition: to criticize someone angrily and harshly

examples.x: The manager berated the employee for the repeated mistakes in the project report.
Frustrated with the situation, he berated himself for not being more careful.
The coach berated the team for their lack of effort during the crucial game.
She couldn't help but berate her friend for breaking a promise.
The teacher berated the students for their disruptive behavior in the classroom.

senses.definition: to strongly and angrily criticize or complain about something

examples.x: Despite the improvements, the customer continued to rail about the service quality.
Maria tends to rail against changes in the workplace, expressing her discontent openly.
The disgruntled employee spent the meeting railing about the company's policies.
Rather than offering constructive feedback, he simply railed against the decision without providing solutions.
Unhappy with the service, the client decided to rail at the restaurant manager.
The parent didn't hesitate to rail at the school administration for their handling of a bullying incident.

senses.definition: to severely criticize, often with the intention of correcting someone's behavior or actions

examples.x: The teacher chastised the student for disrupting the class with loud behavior.
The manager decided to chastise the employee for consistently arriving late to work.
The coach chastised the team for their lack of effort during the crucial match.
The parent chastised the child for not completing homework assignments on time.
The supervisor had to chastise the team members for failing to follow safety protocols in the workplace.

senses.definition: to criticize someone for doing or saying something that one believes to be wrong

examples.x: The teacher upbraided the student for cheating on the exam.
The manager upbraided the team for their failure to meet deadlines.
Displeased with the service, the customer upbraided the waiter for the mistakes in the order.
She upbraided herself for not preparing adequately for the important presentation.
The coach upbraided the players for their lack of dedication during practice.

senses.definition: to speak loudly, expressing strong opinions or complaints

examples.x: The customer began to rant about the poor service, expressing frustration with the long wait and unhelpful staff.
During the meeting, the manager started to rant about the lack of teamwork, emphasizing the need for improvement.
The employee used the meeting to rant about the lack of workplace transparency, emphasizing the need for open communication.
During the class discussion, the student started to rant about the unfairness of the grading system, passionately sharing their grievances.

senses.definition: to make objections, often over small details without a good reason

examples.x: Some coworkers tend to cavil about office temperatures, finding fault with the thermostat setting.
Instead of enjoying the scenic hike, he would cavil at the trail's difficulty level.
Even after a successful event, there were those who continued to cavil at the choice of music.
While most appreciated the effort, a few would cavil about the color scheme chosen for the project.

senses.definition: to express one's disagreement, refusal, or reluctance

examples.x: When asked to work overtime, Sarah demurred, citing family commitments.
The committee member demurred at the proposed budget, expressing concerns about its feasibility.
Instead of openly agreeing, John demurred and suggested exploring alternative solutions.
She demurred at the idea of endorsing the policy, raising ethical concerns.
He has demurred on accepting the promotion, unsure if he's ready for the responsibility.

senses.definition: to complain in a persistent and annoying manner

examples.x: Despite the pleasant weather, Mark continued to whinge about the lack of shade at the picnic.
Instead of enjoying the trip, Emily spent the entire car ride whinging about the uncomfortable seats.
The teacher had little patience for students who would constantly whinge about homework assignments.
Even with the upgraded office facilities, some employees continued to whinge about minor inconveniences.
Jenny's friends avoided inviting her to outings because she tended to whinge about every little detail.

senses.definition: to express dissatisfaction in a way that is annoying or repetitive

examples.x: Instead of finding solutions, Mark continued to bleat about the challenges without taking any action.
During the meeting, some employees began to bleat about the new office policies.
Jane's tendency to bleat about minor inconveniences made it difficult for her coworkers to work in peace.

senses.definition: to express one's dissatisfaction about something, often informally

examples.x: Sarah likes to beef about her workload but rarely takes on extra responsibilities.
Instead of constantly beefing, let's focus on finding solutions to the problem.
Tom tends to beef about trivial matters, creating unnecessary tension at home.
Rather than beefing about the situation, it's more productive to communicate and seek resolution.
The journalist fulminated against the government's handling of the crisis, accusing officials of incompetence.
She fulminated against her opponent's policies in a fiery speech.
The activist fulminated against corporate greed, denouncing companies for prioritizing profits over social responsibility.
The union leader fulminated against the employer's proposed cuts, declaring them an attack on workers' rights.
The politician fulminated against the opposition party, accusing them of spreading lies and misinformation.

word_list.pronunciation: fˈaɪnd fˈɑːlt wɪð ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to identify or point out flaws, errors, or shortcomings in someone or something

examples.x: Instead of appreciating the effort her coworker put into the project, Jane constantly finds fault with the way he organized the data.
John has a tendency to find fault with every restaurant they visit, making it challenging to choose a place to dine.
Even though the play received critical acclaim, some reviewers still managed to find fault with the production's minor technical flaws.
Mary always finds fault with her younger brother, criticizing his choices and decisions.
Sarah's habit of finding fault with her friends' plans makes it challenging for them to organize group outings.
